examQuestions.explanation: CN II (Optic) is tested by: visual acuity (Snellen chart), visual field testing (confrontation), and pupillary light reflex (afferent limb). Swinging flashlight test assesses for RAPD. The optic nerve is special sensory for sight. Facial sensation/jaw tests CN V; forehead/eye closure tests CN VII; gag reflex tests CN IX/X.

examQuestions.question: A bright light is shone into the LEFT eye. Which of the following describes the NORMAL response?
options: Only the left pupil constricts (direct reflex only)
Only the right pupil constricts (consensual only)
Both pupils dilate transiently then constrict
examQuestions.explanation: In a normal pupillary light reflex, shining light in one eye produces BILATERAL pupil constriction: direct reflex in the illuminated eye AND consensual (indirect) reflex in the contralateral eye. This occurs because the pretectal nucleus receives input from CN II and projects BILATERALLY to BOTH Edinger-Westphal nuclei, activating CN III on both sides.

examQuestions.question: A 62-year-old man with known internal carotid artery aneurysm reports progressive vision loss in his left eye with no pain. Examination reveals reduced left visual acuity and a temporal field defect in the left eye only. The lesion is most likely compressing which structure?
options: Left optic nerve near the optic canal
examQuestions.explanation: Ipsilateral monocular vision loss with a field defect in ONE eye points to a lesion of the optic nerve itself (pre-chiasmal). An ICA aneurysm near the optic canal can compress CN II, causing ipsilateral vision loss, reduced visual acuity, and visual field defects. Chiasmal compression would cause bitemporal defects.

examQuestions.question: A patient presents with complete loss of facial sensation on the right side, weakness of right-sided jaw muscles, and loss of the right corneal reflex. The entire right CN V is likely lesioned. Where would a single lesion MOST LIKELY account for all three findings?
options: At the right foramen ovale only
At the right foramen rotundum only
examQuestions.explanation: The trigeminal (Gasserian/semilunar) ganglion is the single structure that houses the cell bodies for ALL sensory fibers of CN V (V1, V2, V3). A lesion here would cause complete ipsilateral facial sensory loss + loss of the corneal reflex (V1 afferent) + loss of jaw sensation (V3 sensory). Motor fibers of V3 have their cell bodies in the trigeminal motor nucleus in the pons, so motor loss suggests a more extensive or proximal lesion.

examQuestions.question: In an acoustic neuroma, CN VIII dysfunction (hearing loss and balance problems) typically precedes involvement of adjacent cranial nerves CN VII and CN V.
examQuestions.explanation: TRUE. An acoustic neuroma arises from the vestibular division of CN VIII. It initially causes CN VIII dysfunction (unilateral sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, and vestibular ataxia). Only as the tumor enlarges at the cerebellopontine angle does it compress CN VII (facial weakness), CN V (facial numbness), and eventually CN IX.

examQuestions.question: A diabetic CN III palsy is more likely to involve the pupil compared to an aneurysmal CN III palsy.
examQuestions.explanation: FALSE. It is the OPPOSITE. Diabetic (microvascular) CN III palsy typically SPARES the pupil because ischemia affects the central core motor fibers while preserving the peripheral parasympathetic fibers. An aneurysmal CN III palsy (e.g., posterior communicating artery) INVOLVES the pupil (dilates it) because external compression affects the peripheral parasympathetic fibers first.

examQuestions.question: CN IX, CN X, and CN XI all pass through the jugular foramen. A tumor at the jugular foramen would MOST LIKELY produce which combination of deficits?
options: Loss of smell, visual field defects, facial drooping
Loss of gag reflex, uvula deviation, hoarseness, shoulder weakness
Tongue deviation, facial paralysis, hearing loss
examQuestions.explanation: A jugular foramen syndrome would involve CN IX, CN X, and CN XI: CN IX → loss of gag reflex, loss of posterior tongue taste/sensation; CN X → uvula deviation, hoarseness (vocal cord palsy), dysphagia; CN XI → ipsilateral shoulder weakness (trapezius), impaired head rotation (SCM). This combination is called 'Vernet syndrome.'

examQuestions.question: The stapedius muscle is innervated by CN VII. Its function is to ________ ossicular chain vibration, protecting against loud sounds. Damage to CN VII proximal to this branch causes ________ (abnormal sensitivity to sound).
In a CN III palsy from a PCA aneurysm, the pupil is ________ (miotic/mydriatic) because the ________ (parasympathetic/sympathetic) fibers run on the OUTSIDE of the nerve and are compressed first.
The myelin of the optic nerve (CN II) is produced by ________, making it susceptible to ________ diseases such as multiple sclerosis.

When testing a patient for CN XI (Spinal Accessory) function, the examiner asks the patient to shrug their shoulder against resistance. The patient CANNOT shrug the right shoulder. Additionally, the patient has difficulty turning their head to which side?
options: To the right (ipsilateral) — because SCM turns head to the same side
To the left (contralateral) — because SCM turns head to the opposite side
examQuestions.explanation: The right SCM turns the head to the LEFT (contralateral side). With a right CN XI lesion, the right SCM is weak → impaired ability to turn the head to the LEFT. The right trapezius is also weak → cannot shrug the RIGHT shoulder. CN XI controls ipsilateral SCM and trapezius, but SCM rotates the head CONTRALATERALLY.

examQuestions.question: A patient with tertiary syphilis presents with bilateral small, irregular pupils that constrict when focusing on a near object but fail to constrict when a bright light is shone in either eye. Which structure is damaged?
options: Dorsal midbrain at the pretectal region
examQuestions.explanation: Argyll Robertson pupils result from a dorsal midbrain (pretectal) lesion that disrupts the LIGHT REFLEX pathway while sparing the ACCOMMODATION pathway. In neurosyphilis (tabes dorsalis), this pattern is classic. The pretectal nucleus (which relays the light reflex to the Edinger-Westphal nucleus) is damaged, but the accommodation pathway (which bypasses the pretectal nucleus via the visual cortex) remains intact.

examQuestions.question: Which of the following is correct regarding the brainstem origin of cranial nerve nuclei?
options: CN IX and X originate from the midbrain
CN III and IV originate from the pons
CN VII and VIII originate from the medulla
CN III and IV originate from the midbrain
examQuestions.explanation: CN III (Oculomotor) and CN IV (Trochlear) both originate from the MIDBRAIN. CN V–VIII originate from the PONS. CN IX, X, XII originate from the MEDULLA. CN XI originates from the SPINAL CORD. This brainstem localization is critical for lesion analysis.

examQuestions.question: The dilator pupillae muscle is innervated by the parasympathetic nervous system and causes mydriasis when activated.
examQuestions.explanation: FALSE. The dilator pupillae is innervated by the SYMPATHETIC nervous system (via long ciliary nerves from the superior cervical ganglion). Sympathetic activation → dilator pupillae contraction → mydriasis. The SPHINCTER pupillae is innervated by the PARASYMPATHETIC system (CN III) and causes miosis when activated.

examQuestions.question: A patient presents with dysphagia and difficulty elevating the pharynx/larynx during swallowing. However, taste and sensation are intact in the posterior tongue. Which specific component of CN IX is most likely damaged?
examQuestions.explanation: The stylopharyngeus is the ONLY muscle innervated by CN IX motor fibers. It elevates the pharynx and larynx during swallowing. Isolated motor CN IX lesion → dysphagia (difficulty swallowing), particularly with pharyngeal elevation. Sensory and parasympathetic components are spared in this scenario.

examQuestions.question: The tensor tympani muscle, which dampens tympanic membrane vibration, is innervated by CN V3 (mandibular nerve).
examQuestions.explanation: TRUE. The tensor tympani is innervated by the medial pterygoid nerve, a branch of CN V3 (mandibular). It tenses the tympanic membrane to reduce ossicular vibration (protective function). Note that the STAPEDIUS (the other sound-dampening middle ear muscle) is innervated by CN VII.

examQuestions.question: The five main motor branches of CN VII in order from superior to inferior are:
options: Temporal, Zygomatic, Buccal, Marginal mandibular, Cervical
Zygomatic, Temporal, Buccal, Cervical, Marginal mandibular
Cervical, Temporal, Zygomatic, Buccal, Marginal mandibular
examQuestions.explanation: The five major motor branches of CN VII from superior to inferior are: Temporal, Zygomatic, Buccal, Marginal mandibular, Cervical. Mnemonic: 'To Zanzibar By Motor Car.' The temporal branch is most superior (forehead), and cervical branch is most inferior (neck/platysma).

examQuestions.question: Which of the following cranial nerves is BOTH sensory AND motor (mixed)?
examQuestions.explanation: CN V (Trigeminal) and CN VII (Facial) are both mixed nerves — they carry both sensory and motor fibers. CN IX and CN X are also mixed. CN I and II are purely sensory. CN III, IV, VI, XI, XII are purely motor. CN VIII is purely sensory.

examQuestions.question: Bilateral paralysis of the recurrent laryngeal nerve causes ________ (complete voice loss) and ________ (harsh respiratory sound on inspiration).

A 40-year-old woman suddenly develops vertical diplopia after a minor head trauma. She tilts her head to the right to compensate. Examination shows the left eye elevated compared to the right. What is the diagnosis?
options: Left CN IV palsy with left hypertropia
Right CN IV palsy with left hypertropia
examQuestions.explanation: CN IV (Trochlear) palsy causes the affected eye's superior oblique to be paralyzed → the affected eye drifts UP (hypertropia) because the inferior oblique (CN III) is now unopposed. With RIGHT CN IV palsy, the right superior oblique is weak → the right eye is elevated relative to the left → compensatory head tilt to the LEFT (toward the affected side corrects extorsion). Wait — the scenario says head tilt to the right and LEFT eye elevated. Left eye elevated (left hypertropia) + right head tilt = RIGHT CN IV palsy with left hypertropia, as the right eye tilts up. This follows the clinical rule: CN IV palsy = contralateral hypertropia, ipsilateral head tilt corrects.

examQuestions.question: A patient has a lesion at the RIGHT side of the OPTIC TRACT (between chiasm and lateral geniculate nucleus). What visual field defect would be expected?
examQuestions.explanation: The right optic tract carries fibers from the right temporal retina (right eye, right visual field) and the left nasal retina (left eye, right visual field). Wait — correction: the right optic tract carries fibers that process the LEFT visual field (temporal retina of right eye = nasal field; nasal retina of left eye = temporal field — these carry the LEFT visual field). Lesion of the right optic tract → LEFT homonymous hemianopia.

examQuestions.question: A dentist injecting near the mandibular foramen for an inferior alveolar nerve block. Which structures will be anesthetized?
options: Upper teeth, upper lip, and palate
Lower teeth, lower lip, and chin
examQuestions.explanation: The inferior alveolar nerve (branch of CN V3) enters the mandible via the mandibular foramen and supplies the lower teeth, lower lip, and chin (via the mental nerve). Dentists use this block for procedures on mandibular teeth. Upper teeth are supplied by CN V2 (maxillary) — superior alveolar nerves — and are not affected.

examQuestions.question: Match each clinical test to the cranial nerve it primarily evaluates.

pairs.left: Identify a familiar smell with each nostril
Check eye movements and pupil constriction
Ask patient to look down and in (adducted downward gaze)
Ask patient to shrug shoulders and turn head against resistance
Ask patient to stick out tongue

examQuestions.explanation: Clinical testing of cranial nerves requires specific maneuvers: CN I uses smell identification; CN II uses vision, fields, and RAPD test; CN III uses eye movement and pupil; CN IV uses downward gaze; CN XI uses shoulder shrug and head turn; CN XII uses tongue protrusion.

examQuestions.question: The pharyngeal branch of CN X, when injured, causes hoarseness of voice.
examQuestions.explanation: FALSE. The PHARYNGEAL branch of CN X injury causes DYSPHAGIA (difficulty swallowing). HOARSENESS is caused by injury to the RECURRENT LARYNGEAL nerve (a branch of CN X) that innervates the vocal cord muscles. The superior laryngeal nerve injury causes weak, fatigable voice. Precise branch localization matters clinically.

examQuestions.question: The inferior oblique muscle, innervated by CN III, elevates and extorts the eye.
examQuestions.explanation: TRUE. The inferior oblique is innervated by CN III and its primary action is ELEVATION and EXTORSION (lateral rotation) of the eye, particularly when the eye is in the adducted position. It is the primary elevator from the adducted position (when superior rectus is inefficient at elevating). This is tested by asking the patient to look up and out.

examQuestions.question: A lesion isolated to the medulla (not spinal cord) would most likely NOT affect which cranial nerve?
examQuestions.explanation: CN XI (Spinal Accessory) originates from the SPINAL CORD (C1–C5/6), not the medulla. Its nucleus is in the cervical spinal cord. A purely medullary lesion would affect CN IX, X, and XII (which have their nuclei in the medulla) but not CN XI whose nucleus is in the spinal cord.

examQuestions.question: A patient with CN VII palsy has dry eyes. Which branch of CN VII is most likely damaged, and which gland is affected?
examQuestions.explanation: Dry eyes (decreased lacrimation) result from damage to the GREATER PETROSAL NERVE, which carries parasympathetic fibers from CN VII to the LACRIMAL GLAND (via pterygopalatine ganglion). Chorda tympani damage causes loss of taste (anterior tongue) and decreased salivation from submandibular/sublingual glands. Lacrimation is via the greater petrosal nerve.

examQuestions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the sympathetic pathway to the pupil (dilator pupillae)?
options: Hypothalamus → Edinger-Westphal → ciliary ganglion → dilator pupillae
Hypothalamus → ciliospinal center (C8-T2) → superior cervical ganglion → dilator pupillae
Pretectal nucleus → Edinger-Westphal → short ciliary nerves → dilator pupillae
Midbrain → oculomotor nerve → long ciliary nerves → dilator pupillae
examQuestions.explanation: The sympathetic pathway to the pupil is a 3-neuron arc: 1st order: Hypothalamus → ciliospinal center of Budge (C8-T2) → descends in lateral brainstem/spinal cord; 2nd order: ciliospinal center → superior cervical ganglion; 3rd order: along internal carotid → through cavernous sinus → long ciliary nerves → dilator pupillae (and superior tarsal muscle, sweat glands). Disruption at any level = Horner syndrome.

examQuestions.question: All intrinsic and extrinsic muscles of the tongue, including the palatoglossus, are innervated by CN XII (Hypoglossal).
examQuestions.explanation: FALSE. The palatoglossus is the ONE EXCEPTION — it is innervated by CN X (Vagus) via the pharyngeal plexus, NOT by CN XII. All other tongue muscles (intrinsic: superior/inferior longitudinal, transverse, vertical; extrinsic: genioglossus, hyoglossus, styloglossus) are innervated by CN XII.

examQuestions.question: Trigeminal neuralgia (tic douloureux) is characterized by continuous, burning facial pain that does not have trigger points.
examQuestions.explanation: FALSE. Trigeminal neuralgia is characterized by SUDDEN, PAROXYSMAL, ELECTRIC SHOCK-LIKE pain (not continuous burning). The pain is triggered by mild stimulation of TRIGGER POINTS (touching face, eating, brushing teeth). Periods between attacks can be pain-free. Continuous burning pain is more characteristic of atypical facial pain or neuropathic pain conditions.

examQuestions.question: Which of the following pupillary scenarios is most consistent with a CN III efferent lesion (rather than a CN II afferent lesion)?
options: Swinging flashlight shows paradoxical dilation in one eye; other eye normal
Ipsilateral fixed dilated pupil with no direct OR consensual response in that eye; other eye responds normally to direct light
Pupils equal, both react to direct and consensual stimulation
examQuestions.explanation: A CN III (efferent) lesion causes fixed dilation of the ipsilateral pupil. The ipsilateral pupil cannot constrict (no direct reflex, no consensual from other eye's light). However, light in the IPSILATERAL eye still travels via CN II → pretectal nucleus → both Edinger-Westphal nuclei → the CONTRALATERAL eye will still constrict normally (its CN III is intact). This pattern = CN III efferent lesion. RAPD (CN II lesion) shows paradoxical dilation on swing flashlight test, not a fixed dilated pupil.

examQuestions.question: Parotitis (parotid gland inflammation from mumps) can refer pain to which area, and via which nerve?
options: Auricle, external acoustic meatus, temporal region, and TMJ; auriculotemporal nerve (CN V3)
Lower lip and chin; CN V3 inferior alveolar branch
examQuestions.explanation: Parotid gland disease refers pain to the auricle, external acoustic meatus, temporal region, and TMJ because the parotid receives sensory innervation via the AURICULOTEMPORAL NERVE (a branch of CN V3). The parotid gland is in the CN V3 sensory territory. This explains referred otalgia (ear pain) from parotid pathology.

examQuestions.question: CN VII and CN VIII both enter the skull through which opening?
examQuestions.explanation: Both CN VII (Facial) and CN VIII (Vestibulocochlear) enter the skull through the INTERNAL ACOUSTIC MEATUS. This is why acoustic neuromas at this location can eventually compress CN VII as they grow, causing facial weakness in addition to the initial hearing and balance problems.

examQuestions.question: The accommodative response of the lens for near vision involves which of the following changes?
options: Ciliary muscle relaxes → zonular fibers under tension → lens stretches thin
Ciliary muscle contracts → zonular fibers relax → lens thickens (more convex)
Ciliary muscle contracts → zonular fibers tighten → lens stretches thin
Ciliary muscle relaxes → zonular fibers relax → lens thickens spontaneously
examQuestions.explanation: For near vision accommodation: Parasympathetic stimulation (via CN III) causes ciliary muscle to CONTRACT → zonular fibers RELAX → in the absence of tension, the lens's internal elasticity causes it to become MORE SPHERICAL (thicker, more convex) → greater light-bending power for near focus. For distance: ciliary muscle relaxes → zonular fibers tighten → lens stretches thin → less refractive power.

examQuestions.question: A Marcus Gunn pupil indicates a defect in the ________ limb of the pupillary reflex (involving CN ________), while an efferent defect involves CN ________.
CN V has three divisions: V1 exits via the ________, V2 via the ________, and V3 via the ________.
examQuestions.answer: superior orbital fissure; foramen rotundum; foramen ovale
acceptableAnswers: superior orbital fissure; foramen rotundum; foramen ovale

examQuestions.question: A patient has right facial droop SPARING the forehead on the right side, right arm and right leg weakness. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Left hemisphere stroke (upper motor neuron CN VII lesion)
Right CN VII peripheral lesion plus brachial plexus injury
Bilateral CN VII lesion from Lyme disease
examQuestions.explanation: Lower facial weakness with FOREHEAD SPARING indicates a CENTRAL (supranuclear/upper motor neuron) lesion, not a peripheral CN VII lesion. The upper face (frontalis) is spared because it receives bilateral cortical innervation — the intact right hemisphere still drives the right forehead. The associated arm/leg weakness on the same side as the facial droop points to a contralateral (LEFT) hemisphere stroke affecting the corticospinal and corticobulbar tracts.

examQuestions.question: Which of the following is NOT a function of CN X (Vagus)?
options: Sensory from the posterior ear skin
Parasympathetic innervation to the parotid gland
Motor to laryngeal muscles via recurrent laryngeal nerve
examQuestions.explanation: CN X does NOT innervate the parotid gland. The parotid gland parasympathetic supply comes from CN IX via the otic ganglion. CN X provides: parasympathetics to the heart (slows rate), sensory from the external ear skin, and motor to intrinsic laryngeal muscles via the recurrent laryngeal nerve.

examQuestions.question: Which of the following findings would you expect SPECIFICALLY with damage to the EFFERENT limb (CN VII) of the corneal reflex, compared to the afferent limb (CN V1)?
options: Neither eye blinks when either cornea is touched
The affected eye does not blink when either cornea is touched, but the normal eye still blinks
Neither eye blinks when the affected cornea is touched; both eyes blink when the normal cornea is touched
Both eyes blink normally regardless of which cornea is touched
examQuestions.explanation: CN VII (efferent/motor) lesion: The ipsilateral orbicularis oculi is paralyzed → the affected eye CANNOT blink when EITHER cornea is touched (no motor output). The NORMAL eye still blinks normally for both direct and consensual stimulation. CN V1 (afferent/sensory) lesion: Touching the AFFECTED cornea → NEITHER eye blinks; touching the NORMAL cornea → BOTH eyes blink.

examQuestions.question: The olfactory pathway is clinically unique because it projects directly to the limbic system, bypassing the thalamus. Which structure in the limbic system does olfactory information reach, contributing to emotional and memory responses to smell?
options: Amygdala and hippocampus via the piriform cortex
examQuestions.explanation: Olfactory information travels from the olfactory bulb → olfactory tract → primary olfactory cortex (piriform cortex) → amygdala (emotional processing) and hippocampus (memory formation). This direct limbic connection explains why smells powerfully and rapidly trigger emotions, fear responses, and autobiographical memories — without the thalamic 'filter' that other senses use.

examQuestions.question: The levator palpebrae superioris muscle, responsible for eyelid elevation, is innervated by CN III (Oculomotor).
examQuestions.explanation: TRUE. The levator palpebrae superioris is innervated by CN III (Oculomotor). When CN III is damaged, this muscle is paralyzed → ptosis (drooping of the upper eyelid). Note: there is also a superior tarsal muscle (Müller's muscle) that assists in eyelid elevation and is innervated by SYMPATHETICS — its loss in Horner syndrome causes partial (not complete) ptosis.

examQuestions.question: A 68-year-old woman presents with sudden onset of severe headache, left ptosis, and a left dilated non-reactive pupil. Eye movement testing shows the left eye deviated down and laterally. The most urgent next step is imaging for which condition?
examQuestions.explanation: A CN III palsy with pupil involvement (mydriasis, non-reactive) + sudden severe headache ('thunderclap') = posterior communicating artery aneurysm until proven otherwise. This is a neurosurgical emergency. The PCoA runs adjacent to CN III, and an aneurysm compresses the peripheral parasympathetic fibers first, causing mydriasis. MS or ischemic (diabetic) CN III palsy typically SPARES the pupil.

examQuestions.question: The geniculate ganglion is the sensory ganglion of CN VII. Herpes zoster reactivation here causes ________ syndrome, characterized by vesicular rash in the ear, ipsilateral facial palsy, and sometimes hearing loss.

After a car accident, a patient develops clear fluid dripping from their nose. The fluid is sent to the lab and found to contain beta-2 transferrin (a CSF marker). Which accompanying neurological deficit would most strongly suggest a cribriform plate fracture rather than another skull base fracture?
examQuestions.explanation: Anosmia (loss of smell) strongly suggests a cribriform plate fracture because the olfactory nerve fibers pass through this structure. CSF rhinorrhea from a cribriform plate fracture is often accompanied by anosmia. Facial paralysis suggests temporal bone (CN VII) involvement; hearing loss suggests CN VIII involvement; diplopia suggests cavernous sinus or orbital injury.

examQuestions.question: Which of the following pupillary findings is associated with CN III nerve palsy due to COMPRESSION (e.g., aneurysm) versus CN III palsy due to ISCHEMIA (e.g., diabetes)?
options: Compression: pupil spared; Ischemia: pupil involved (dilated)
Compression: pupil dilated (involved); Ischemia: pupil spared
examQuestions.explanation: Compression (e.g., PCA aneurysm): parasympathetic fibers run on the OUTSIDE of CN III → compressed FIRST → mydriasis (pupil INVOLVED). Ischemia (e.g., diabetic microvascular): central core motor fibers are more metabolically demanding → ischemia affects core first → parasympathetic fibers preserved → pupil SPARED. This distinction is critical: pupil involvement in CN III palsy = rule out aneurysm emergently.

examQuestions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the pathway of the corneal reflex?
options: Touch cornea → CN VII afferent → brainstem → CN V1 efferent → blink
Touch cornea → CN V1 afferent → brainstem → CN VII efferent → bilateral orbicularis oculi contraction
Touch cornea → CN II afferent → brainstem → CN III efferent → blink
Touch cornea → CN IX afferent → brainstem → CN X efferent → blink
examQuestions.explanation: The corneal reflex pathway: Touching the cornea → CN V1 (ophthalmic) carries the afferent signal to the spinal trigeminal nucleus → brainstem interneurons → bilateral facial motor nuclei → CN VII (facial) efferent → bilateral orbicularis oculi contraction → bilateral blinking (direct in the touched eye, consensual in the other eye).

examQuestions.question: During a carotid endarterectomy, the surgeon inadvertently injures CN XII. Which immediate functional deficit would the patient demonstrate upon awakening?
options: Hoarse voice from vocal cord paralysis
Loss of taste on ipsilateral tongue
Tongue deviation toward the injured side and dysarthria
Inability to shrug the ipsilateral shoulder
examQuestions.explanation: CN XII (Hypoglossal) innervates the tongue muscles. Injury during carotid endarterectomy (a known risk, as CN XII crosses near the carotid bifurcation) causes tongue deviation TOWARD the lesion and dysarthria (slurred speech from impaired articulation). Hoarse voice implicates RLN (CN X branch); taste loss implicates CN VII chorda tympani; shoulder weakness implicates CN XI.

examQuestions.question: A patient has CN X injury at the level of the pharyngeal branch ONLY. Which finding would be expected?
options: Uvula deviation with intact vocal cords
examQuestions.explanation: The pharyngeal branch of CN X innervates the pharyngeal muscles (except stylopharyngeus) and is responsible for pharyngeal constriction during swallowing. An isolated pharyngeal branch injury causes DYSPHAGIA (difficulty swallowing) without hoarseness or aphonia (which require recurrent laryngeal nerve injury). Uvula deviation would also occur because uvular muscles are affected.

examQuestions.question: To best test CN IV (Trochlear), the examiner should ask the patient to look in which direction?
examQuestions.explanation: CN IV innervates the superior oblique, whose primary action is depression and intorsion from the adducted position. To best isolate and test the superior oblique, ask the patient to look DOWN and IN (depression from adduction). Inability or diplopia in this gaze direction indicates CN IV palsy. Patients often complain of diplopia going down stairs (looking down while the eye is somewhat adducted).

examQuestions.question: Which of the following is the MOST important immediate treatment concern in Bell's Palsy regarding the eye?
options: Preventing corneal exposure injury by lubricating drops and eye taping
Restoring taste sensation with zinc supplements
examQuestions.explanation: The most urgent management concern in Bell's Palsy is PROTECTING THE CORNEA. Orbicularis oculi paralysis prevents complete eye closure (lagophthalmos) → corneal exposure, drying, and ulceration. This is managed with artificial tears, lubricating eye ointments at night, and eye patching/taping when sleeping. Untreated, it can lead to permanent corneal scarring and vision loss.

examQuestions.question: Which extraocular muscles receive their innervation exclusively from CN III (Oculomotor)?
options: Superior rectus, inferior rectus, medial rectus, inferior oblique, levator palpebrae
examQuestions.explanation: CN III innervates four of the six extraocular muscles (superior rectus, inferior rectus, medial rectus, inferior oblique) plus the levator palpebrae superioris. CN IV innervates the superior oblique. CN VI innervates the lateral rectus. Remember: LR6-SO4-R3.

examQuestions.question: Which of the following is the key distinguishing feature between peripheral CN VII palsy (Bell's palsy) and central CN VII weakness from a stroke?
options: Presence of taste loss — only in central lesions
Involvement of the forehead — affected in peripheral, spared in central
Presence of ptosis — only in peripheral CN VII lesions
Hyperacusis — present only in central CN VII lesions
examQuestions.explanation: The KEY distinction is forehead involvement: PERIPHERAL CN VII (Bell's palsy) involves BOTH upper and lower face, including the forehead (frontalis is weak — no forehead wrinkling). CENTRAL (supranuclear/stroke) CN VII lesion affects only the LOWER face; the forehead is SPARED because it receives bilateral cortical innervation from both hemispheres.

examQuestions.question: A medical student creates the mnemonic 'Some Say Marry Money But My Brother Says Big Brains Matter More' to remember the functional types of cranial nerves. What does 'B' stand for in this mnemonic?
examQuestions.explanation: In the mnemonic for cranial nerve types: S=Sensory, M=Motor, B=Both (mixed sensory AND motor). The nerves with B (Both) are: CN V (Trigeminal), CN VII (Facial), CN IX (Glossopharyngeal), and CN X (Vagus). These carry afferent sensory AND efferent motor fibers.

examQuestions.question: Visual field testing shows that a patient has lost vision in the left temporal field (left eye) and the right nasal field (right eye). At which point in the visual pathway does this defect localize?
examQuestions.explanation: Loss of the left temporal field (carried by nasal retinal fibers of the left eye) and the right nasal field (carried by nasal retinal fibers of the right eye — which cross at the chiasm) = bitemporal field defect. Wait — reread: left TEMPORAL field = temporal retina of left eye (uncrossed) + nasal retina of right eye (crossed). Selective loss of NASAL retinal fibers from both eyes (crossing fibers) occurs at the OPTIC CHIASM. This is hemianopia from chiasm compression.

examQuestions.question: Match each drug/substance to its effect on pupil size and the mechanism.

pairs.right: Mydriasis — blocks norepinephrine reuptake → excess adrenergic stimulation of dilator
Miosis — relieves inhibition on parasympathetic fibers → ACh release → sphincter activation
Miosis — inhibit acetylcholinesterase → excess ACh → sustained sphincter activation

examQuestions.explanation: Pupillary pharmacology: parasympathomimetics (pilocarpine, organophosphates, opioids) → miosis; anticholinergics (atropine) → mydriasis; sympathomimetics (cocaine, amphetamines) → mydriasis. Knowing the mechanism helps predict effects in toxicology scenarios.
